briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. I will now begin the climate briefing for March 26, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis and Normalized Extreme Weather Events
sections.content: The Korean Peninsula is facing extreme climate phenomena, with the climate crisis becoming normalized and weather polarization reaching its peak [1]. Warnings have emerged that even with a 2℃ rise in global temperature, the risks of drought and heavy rainfall will be greater than anticipated [6], and in 2025, large-scale wildfires, prolonged heatwaves, and localized torrential downpours occurred in succession [21, 27]. Last year, various abnormal weather events, including heatwaves, heavy rainfall, and droughts, were confirmed, recording the largest-ever damage [28, 30, 35, 38], actualizing the perception that 'seasons have collapsed' [43]. Internationally, concerns are also being raised that climate change is collapsing modern agriculture and pushing our world into unknown territory [147, 157].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Expansion of Domestic Carbon Neutrality and Climate Change Response Efforts
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts to respond to climate change are expanding. Rural areas in Jeonbuk have finalized their climate change response action plans, accelerating carbon reduction [4, 17, 23, 32, 37], and Uljin County is exploring climate change adaptation in the agricultural sector, including promoting 'delaying rice seedling transplantation by 10 days' [8, 14]. The Korea National Arboretum is conducting plant phenology monitoring education [7, 13], and Gangbuk-gu is implementing carbon neutrality education using smart farms [47, 62, 96], thus expanding the role of educational and research institutions. Furthermore, movements encouraging citizen participation are active, such as Yeosu City recruiting volunteers for the UNFCCC Climate Week [2, 20, 24, 29, 34].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Technological Innovation
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy for achieving carbon neutrality is accelerating. The government and ruling party have decided to expand budgets for supporting household renewable energy supply and promoting public transportation [117, 121, 126, 139, 140], and a plan to include 'temperature difference energy' in renewable energy is also being pursued [111]. Hyundai Engineering & Construction is expanding its decarbonization business with SMRs and renewable energy [115, 137], and Gyeongju City is accelerating efforts to attract the first SMR, dubbed a key to carbon neutrality [45, 53, 60, 98, 103]. Furthermore, Samsung C&T has completed the nation's first green hydrogen production facility utilizing 100% renewable energy [142], and LS Electric has won the 'GEAN Award' for its contribution to carbon neutrality based on smart energy technology [69, 75, 84, 89, 97, 102], demonstrating active technological innovation and investment by companies.

sections.title: 🌍 International Trends and Policy Challenges
sections.content: Internationally, issues of responsibility for climate change and policy consistency are emerging as key challenges. A study has revealed that the United States and China have caused an estimated $19 trillion in economic damage worldwide over the past 30 years due to greenhouse gas emissions [31, 144], emphasizing the accountability of major emitting countries. Domestically, a lack of policy consistency regarding the Financial Services Commission's ESG disclosure roadmap has been pointed out, prompting a call for a complete re-evaluation [19], and during the public discourse process for the Carbon Neutrality Act, the agenda deliberation committee collectively resigned due to path conflicts [44, 51], indicating difficulties in policy implementation. Furthermore, environmental organizations are increasingly demanding transparent implementation plans from corporations, such as urging Hyundai Steel to disclose a concrete carbon neutrality roadmap [48, 49, 65, 100].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted the severity of the climate crisis, along with various domestic and international efforts to overcome it. This is a time when sustained attention and action become even more crucial. This concludes today's climate briefing.
